The standard framework: CO₂ from burning biomass is counted as zero at the point of combustion.

Work in pairs:

List the assumptions this framework makes
For each: is it testable? What evidence would challenge it?
Which assumptions, if wrong, would most change the conclusion?



[15 min] This directly feeds Week 3 app (scenario analysis) and Week 4 (the briefing).
Expected assumptions: trees regrow (timescale?), supply chain emissions are negligible, land use doesn’t change, the alternative fuel would have been burned anyway.
The “if wrong, most changes the conclusion” question is the key one. It teaches students to prioritise assumptions by impact.

title: How confident should we be?
text:
What did you find?
Collect 3–4 assumptions from the room.


Draw these out and write them up. Students will need them for the application session (scenario analysis) and the Week 4 briefing.

title: How confident should we be?
text:
“What assumptions are we making?”
This is the question that separates good analysis from bad.

title: How confident should we be?
text:
Key points

All measurements have uncertainty — the question is how much
Systematic error is more dangerous than random error
The biomass “zero” is an assumption, not a measurement
Next step: what happens when we change the assumptions?

title: How confident should we be?
text:
Exit ticket
A lifecycle assessment reports that UK biomass electricity produces 0 gCO₂/kWh. What is the most important thing to check before accepting this number?











PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Correct answer: B — What system boundary and timescale the assessment used.
The “zero” is not a measurement — it is a policy assumption that depends entirely on what is included (combustion only? transport? processing? regrowth?) and over what time horizon.
D (peer review) is a reasonable instinct but wrong as the most important thing — a peer-reviewed paper can still use a narrow system boundary.
This question directly tests whether students have internalised “What assumptions are we making?” as a reflexive question.

title: How confident should we be?
text:
Next time
Application session: “Changing the assumptions”
You’ll take the data and ask: does the answer change when we change the inputs?


Students should arrive ready to do arithmetic in R — multiplying emission factors by generation volumes under different scenarios.

title: Making the case
text:
The journey so far

Week 1: Is biomass carbon-neutral? (Curiosity)
Week 2: The numbers are interesting (Discovery)
Week 3: The answer depends on the assumptions (Doubt)


Week 4: Write it up. Make the case. And learn to spot when someone else isn’t making it honestly.


[5 min] Frame the arc. Students have gone from curiosity to evidence to uncertainty. Now: communication and judgement.

title: Making the case
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Making the case
text:
Who is your reader?
A policy-maker: busy, not a specialist, needs to make a decision.

What they need:

A clear bottom line
Evidence they can trust
Honest acknowledgement of uncertainty



[20 min block] Writing for non-specialists. This is the communication strand coming to a head.

title: Making the case
text:
Structure of a policy briefing



Section
Purpose




Summary
The answer — first. Reader could stop here.


Evidence
Figures, data, analysis


Caveats
What’s uncertain, what depends on assumptions


Conclusions
Recommendation, with caveats attached




Compare to an academic paper: background first, answer last.
A briefing is the opposite — answer first, evidence after.


Show 1–2 real policy briefing examples if available (one-pagers). Point out the structure.
Key principle: “Your reader should be able to stop after the summary and still know the main message.”

title: Making the case
text:
The summary test

If your reader stops after the first paragraph, do they know:

What you investigated?
What you found?
What you recommend?




This is the standard for a good summary. If any of the three are missing, the summary needs work.

title: Making the case
text:
10 minutes
Write a 3–4 sentence summary of your biomass findings, aimed at a government minister.

Then we’ll share a few.

Is the message clear? Is the uncertainty honest? Would a minister know what to do?



[15 min including sharing] Students write, then 2–3 volunteers share. Class gives quick feedback using the three questions above.

title: Making the case
text:
The heart of this session
I’m going to show you the same data presented two ways.
Both are technically accurate.
One is honest. The other is not.


[25 min block] This is the exemplar reveal. Use the faithful and traitor AI environmental impact reports.
Take your time. Let students react. The moment they realise the traitor report “feels” more convincing is pedagogically powerful.

title: Making the case
text:
Two exemplar reports

Faithful report
Balanced evidence. Honest caveats. Uncertain where the data are uncertain.
Traitor report
Selective evidence. Misleading framing. Confident where it shouldn’t be.


Let’s look at the techniques.


Show both reports (or excerpts) side by side. Students read briefly, then you walk through the techniques.

title: Making the case
text:
Technique 1: Cherry-picking
Choose the number that tells your story.

Example: AI energy per query — 0.3 Wh (a Google search) vs 18.9 Wh (a complex AI task). Same topic, 60× difference, depending on which you cite.


The traitor report cited the smaller number to downplay AI’s energy cost. The faithful report cited the range.

title: Making the case
text:
Technique 2: Invalid chart type
A pie chart of things that don’t form a whole.

Or a bar chart where the y-axis starts at 900.


The chart is “technically correct” but visually lies.

title: Making the case
text:
Technique 3: Cross-scale conflation
Compare a small thing to a big thing without adjusting the scale.

Example: “Global AI uses less electricity than UK households.”
Both are true numbers — but the comparison is meaningless without matching the scales (global vs national).

title: Making the case
text:
Technique 4: Omission
What you don’t show matters as much as what you do.

Example: Omit the supply chain, the payback period, or the alternative scenario. The remaining evidence looks cleaner — and more convincing.


Don’t reveal all the techniques in the teaching notes — save some for the application session reveal, when students have had a chance to spot them during peer review.

title: Making the case
text:
The lesson

The most dangerous misinformation is technically sourced.
You can’t just fact-check the numbers — you have to audit the choices.



This connects everything: Week 1 (falsifiability), Week 2 (“Compared to what?”), Week 3 (“What assumptions?”).
The skills for spotting deception are the same skills for avoiding it accidentally.

title: Making the case
text:
Spot the omission
A deliberately incomplete data summary from the biomass case.

What’s been left out? How does the omission change the impression?



[10 min] Small-group discussion. Show a summary that omits the payback period or the supply chain. Students identify what’s missing and how it changes the conclusion.

title: Making the case
text:
Principles (revisited)

Every figure needs a “so what” — what should the reader take from it?
Don’t show everything. Show the thing that matters.
Captions tell the story, not just the axes.
Label directly rather than relying on legends.



[20 min block] Practical guidance for the figures going into the briefing. Show before/after examples.

title: Making the case
text:
Before and after

Before

8 fuel types, no legend order
Y-axis label: “twh”
No caption
Grey background

After

Top 4 fuels, ordered by value
Y-axis: “Generation (TWh)”
Caption: “Biomass has replaced coal…”
Clean white background




If possible, show the actual figures side by side. The contrast should be stark.

title: Making the case
text:
Your assignment
Write a ~2-page policy briefing answering:
“Is UK biomass electricity carbon-neutral?”

Due: committed to your GitHub repo by the end of the application session.
Structure: summary → evidence (with figures) → caveats → conclusions.


[10 min] Hand out the assignment details. This is formative — it doesn’t count toward the final grade, but it’s essential practice.

title: Making the case
text:
Peer review
Each of you will be assigned to review someone else’s briefing.
You’ll file GitHub Issues with structured feedback:

Clarity, evidence, uncertainty, trust


And you’ll make a trust judgement: faithful or suspect?

title: Making the case
text:
The traitors
Some of you will write deliberately misleading briefings.

If you want to be a traitor, let me know privately.
Traitors receive a briefing sheet with specific techniques to use.


Everyone else: read carefully. Not everything is what it seems.


Traitors self-select (aim for 15–20%). They receive the traitor briefing sheet privately (email or DM during break).
If too few volunteer, privately recruit. If too many, some can be “double agents” (write faithful briefings but think they’re traitors).
Keep this light and fun. The traitors are doing the class a favour.

title: Making the case
text:
Next time
Application session: “Write, review, reveal”
Three phases:

Write your briefing (45 min)
Review someone else’s (30 min)
The reveal (25 min)


Come ready to write. Your figures from Weeks 2–3 are your evidence.


Students should have their scenario analysis and figures ready to reuse. The application session is about synthesis and writing, not new analysis.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The shift

Phase 1 (done ✓)
Scaffolded mini-project.
Same data, same question.
Formative.
Phase 2 (starts now)
Your investigation.
Your question, your data.
Summative — 30% of module grade.



[5 min] Set the tone. Phase 2 is where students take ownership. The skills are the same; the stakes are higher.
Briefly outline the assessment: individual 4-page policy report based on group project work.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The assessment
Individual 4-page policy report.

Based on your group’s investigation
Same structure as Phase 1: summary → evidence → caveats → conclusions
Due after Week 10


Your commit history matters — it shows your individual contribution.


The report is individual even though the project is group-based. Students need to understand: the group collects and analyses data together; each person writes their own report.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The logic of comparison
You can’t know if something is big unless you measure something else.


[20 min block] Experimental design fundamentals.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Controls
What you hold constant so you can isolate the variable of interest.

Example: comparing wind farm output across sites.



Control for
Why




Turbine model
Different models have different capacities


Terrain
Ridgetop vs valley affects wind speed


Grid curtailment
Some farms are told to stop generating





What can’t you control? Weather. Maintenance schedules.


Geoscience angle: in field sciences we rarely get true experiments. We observe natural variation and try to isolate factors. This makes design more important, not less.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Treatment vs control groups

Lab sciences
Treatment group gets the intervention.
Control group doesn’t.
Random assignment.
Field sciences
“Treatment” is often a natural condition.
“Control” is a comparison group.
You can’t randomise geology.



Because we can’t randomise, we need to think harder about what might confound our comparisons. That’s next.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The scenario


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Ground Investigation Report — Proposed Wind Farm, County Durham
HolmesCo drilled 3 boreholes in the valley where access roads already exist. All three encountered competent sandstone.
Conclusion: “Bedrock across the site is suitable for turbine foundations.”



What’s wrong with this? Work in pairs — 5 minutes.



[15 min] Expected problems: (a) boreholes are in the valley, turbines are on ridgetops; (b) 3 is tiny; (c) convenience sampling;

no control site. What would a better investigation look like?

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The problems

Selection bias: sampled where it was convenient, not representative
Wrong location: boreholes in the valley, turbines on the ridgetops
Tiny sample: 3 boreholes for a multi-km² site
No comparison: how do we know this is unusually good (or bad)?

title: Designing your investigation
text:
What is a confounder?
A variable that is correlated with both the treatment and the outcome.
You can’t tell which caused the effect.


[20 min block] Confounders are the hidden trap in observational studies.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Classic examples








Observation
Apparent cause
Real cause (confounder)




Ice cream sales ↑, drowning ↑
Ice cream causes drowning?
Hot weather


More solar panels → higher GDP
Solar causes wealth?
Latitude, governance, investment


Deeper boreholes → higher temp
Depth causes heat?
Location (geothermal area)




Correlation is not causation — because of confounders.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
How to handle confounders

Randomisation — when possible (rare in geoscience)
Stratification — compare within subgroups
Matching — pair similar observations
Acknowledgement — when all else fails, be honest about what you can’t control



Option 4 is the most common in geoscience and policy analysis. Honesty about confounders is itself a research skill.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Three scenarios
For each: identify the confounder and suggest how to address it.

1. “Communities near wind farms report more headaches than communities without wind farms.”


Confounders: rural/urban, age, awareness/nocebo effect, reporting bias.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Scenario 2
2. “Countries with higher nuclear capacity have lower carbon emissions per capita.”

Confounders: GDP, industrialisation stage, energy mix decisions driven by geography and politics.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Scenario 3
3. “Students who use AI assistants score higher on coding assignments.”

Confounders: prior coding experience, motivation, time spent on assignments.


[15 min total] Quick discussion for each. Don’t linger — the point is pattern recognition, not exhaustive analysis.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
How to choose your sample








Strategy
How
When




Random
Every unit has equal probability
Gold standard; often impractical


Stratified
Sample within each subgroup
Ensures representation


Systematic
Every nth unit
Regular grids, monitoring stations


Convenience
Whatever’s easiest
HolmesCo’s default





[20 min block] Practical sampling. The key message: your sampling strategy is a decision you must justify.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
The HolmesCo default
“We sampled these because they were there.”

Honest — but weak.



Your sampling strategy is a decision you must justify in your report.



This connects directly to Phase 2: students will need to explain why they chose their data sources and whether their sample is representative.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Choose your topic
Browse the available project topics.

Form provisional groups (3–4 people).


Pick a candidate topic. Then sketch:

Question — what are you investigating?
Data — what would you need?
Comparison — what’s your control?
Confounders — what should you worry about?



[15 min] Groups form and discuss. Each group shares one sentence. Topics don’t need to be final — that happens in the app session.

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Key points

Good design = clear comparison + controlled confounders
Sampling is a choice you must justify
HolmesCo’s mistakes are easy to make — watch for them in your own work

title: Designing your investigation
text:
Next time
Application session: “Your question, your plan”

Finalise groups and topics
Set up your group GitHub repo
Write a one-page research plan



Students should come to the application session with a topic preference and at least a tentative group.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Where we are

Week 3: the logic of hypothesis testing (conceptual)
Week 5: experimental design, controls, confounders


This week: the formal machinery. You’ll learn to run a t-test in R.


And then you’ll learn why it can mislead you.


[5 min] Frame the session: first half is mechanics, second half is the most important lesson of the course.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
What does a t-test do?
Compares two group means and asks:

“Is this difference larger than we’d expect by chance?”



[25 min block] The t-test, step by step, with a live demo.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
The logic

Assume no difference (null hypothesis)
Calculate how far apart the means are, relative to the variability (→ t-statistic)
Ask: if there were really no difference, how often would we see a t this extreme? (→ p-value)

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Live demo

🖥️ Switching to WebR

t.test(temperature ~ site, data = borehole)


Use the borehole temperature dataset. Show the output: t-statistic, df, p-value, confidence interval.
Interpret aloud: “The mean difference is X°C, with a 95% CI of [Y, Z]. The p-value is 0.03, meaning we’d see a difference this large about 3% of the time if there were truly no difference.”

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Reading the output
t = 2.34, df = 18, p-value = 0.031
95% CI: [0.12, 2.34]




Element
Meaning




t-statistic
How many SEs apart are the means


p-value
How surprising, if H₀ is true


95% CI
Plausible range for the true difference





Emphasise: the CI is often more useful than the p-value. It tells you the size of the effect, not just whether it exists.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Write one sentence
I’m showing you R output from a t-test.
Write one sentence interpreting this result for a policy audience.

Then we’ll compare.


[10 min] Show pre-prepared output on screen. Students write, then share.
Common mistake to watch for: “the p-value means there’s a 3% chance the null is true.” Flag this — it’s exactly the confusion block 3 will address.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
A common mistake

“p = 0.03 means there’s a 3% chance the null is true.”


Wrong. But extremely common. We’ll come back to this.


Plant the seed now. The base rate fallacy in block 3 will explain why this interpretation is wrong.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Every test rests on assumptions
For the t-test:

Independence — observations don’t influence each other
Normality — data within each group are roughly normal
Equal variance — groups have similar spread (Student’s t; Welch’s t relaxes this)



[20 min block] Assumptions, how to check, what to do when violated.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
How to check



Assumption
Check




Normality
Histogram, QQ plot, shapiro.test()


Equal variance
Side-by-side boxplots, Levene’s test


Independence
Think about the study design

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
When assumptions are violated
A skewed variable can produce misleading p-values.



HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Statistical Analysis — Soil Permeability
t-test on raw values: p = 0.04 → “Significant difference!”
t-test on log-transformed values: p = 0.23 → “No significant difference.”
The “significant” result was an artefact of skewness.



This is a vivid example. HolmesCo tested permeability (always log-normal) without transforming. The p-value changed from 0.04 to 0.23 — the “discovery” vanished.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
In WebR
Given a dataset:

Make histograms of each group
Decide whether to transform
Run t.test() before and after transformation
Compare: did the conclusion change?



[10 min] Quick hands-on exercise. The WebR page has the scaffolded version with hints.

title: Testing hypotheses
section: The most important 25 minutes of the course
text:
The most important 25 minutes of the course
I’m going to show you why a statistically significant result can still be wrong most of the time.


[25 min block] Take your time. This is the intellectual centrepiece of the module. Let the numbers sink in.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Warm-up: Monty Hall
Some of you simulated the Monty Hall problem in first-year Python.

Switching wins 2/3 of the time. But your gut says 50/50.


Why? Because your gut ignores the information the host gave you when they opened a door.


That confusion — between P(win) and P(win | what you now know) — is exactly what this block is about.


Brief connection. Don’t re-derive Monty Hall. The point: conditional probability is unintuitive, and this has serious consequences.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
The medical test
A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people.
A test is 99% accurate (99% sensitivity, 99% specificity).
You test positive.


What’s the probability you have the disease?

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Cast your vote











PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Activate the poll before showing this slide. Use a numeric open-ended response so answers appear as a histogram.
Most students will cluster around 95–99%. A few may guess lower. The spread makes the reveal (“it’s 9%”) land much harder — they can see on screen that almost everyone got it wrong.
Wait until responses plateau, then advance to the arithmetic.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
The arithmetic
Out of 100,000 people:




Has disease
No disease
Total




Test positive
99
999
1,098


Test negative
1
98,901
98,902


Total
100
99,900
100,000




P(disease | positive) = 99 / 1,098 ≈ 9%


Not 99%. Nine percent.


Let this land. Pause. “Why is it so low?”

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Why is it so low?
Because the disease is rare.
The base rate matters enormously.

Even a very accurate test produces many false positives when the condition is uncommon.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
HolmesCo strikes gold


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Press Release — Major Gold Discovery in County Durham!
“Our geochemical assay (95% accuracy) detected gold in 50 out of 10,000 soil samples. Confirmed multi-element anomaly!”



Work through the numbers on the board or next slide.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
The reality
Suppose 5 in 10,000 samples are actually gold-bearing.





Gold present
No gold
Total




Assay positive
~5
~500
~505


Assay negative
~0
~9,495
~9,495





HolmesCo’s 50 positives? Almost certainly all false.


The press release is nonsense.


The 50 “hits” is actually fewer than the ~500 expected false positives. If anything, the assay is underperforming.
This is the Week 6 equivalent of the traitor reveal.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Connection to p-values
A p-value tells you: P(data | H₀)
How surprising is this data if the null is true?

What you actually want: P(H₀ | data)
How likely is the null to be true given this data?



These are not the same thing. The difference depends on the base rate.



Key sentence: “If you’re testing an implausible hypothesis, a significant p-value is probably a false positive. If you’re testing something well-supported, a significant result is more convincing.”

title: Testing hypotheses
text: The new refrain

title: Testing hypotheses
section: “How plausible was this before we tested?”
text:
“How plausible was this before we tested?”


This is the Bayesian insight without the formula. The habit of asking about prior plausibility is what matters.
This also sets up Week 7: if you run 20 tests, the base rate for any individual hypothesis being true is low → false positives accumulate.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
For your project
Discuss with your group:

What comparison will you test?
What’s your null hypothesis?
What’s your prior expectation — plausible or a long shot?
What assumptions should you check?

Write 3–4 sentences.


[10 min] This feeds directly into the application session.

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Key points

A t-test compares two groups — but check the assumptions first
A p-value is P(data | H₀), not P(H₀ | data)
The base rate determines whether a “significant” result is real
Always ask: “How plausible was this before I tested?”

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Exit ticket
A t-test comparing two groups returns p = 0.04. Which statement is correct?











PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Correct answer: B — P(data | H₀), not P(H₀ | data).
A is the classic confusion of P(data | H₀) with P(H₀ | data). Very common, even among experienced researchers.
C conflates statistical significance with practical or scientific significance — and ignores the base rate argument from this session.
D inverts the conditional probability (like A, but from the other direction).
If many students answer A or D, revisit the medical test example: “If the disease has a 1-in-1000 base rate, a positive test is probably still a false positive — even though p < 0.05.”

title: Testing hypotheses
text:
Next time
Application session: “First tests”
You’ll run your first real tests on your project data.

Remember: a significant p-value is the start of the conversation, not the end.


Students should come ready to work on their project data. Groups may need to do data cleaning before testing.

title: Comparing groups
text:
Where we are
Last week: t-tests for two groups. The base rate fallacy.

This week: what happens when you have more than two groups — and more than one test.


[5 min] Quick recap. Any lingering questions from last week’s base rate discussion?

title: Comparing groups
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Comparing groups
text:
The problem
Three groups (e.g., wind farms in three regions).
You could run three pairwise t-tests: A vs B, A vs C, B vs C.

Each test has a 5% false positive rate.


Three tests: P(at least one false positive) = 1 − 0.95³ ≈ 14%
Ten tests: 40%
Twenty tests: 64%


[20 min block] Multiple comparisons. Build to the HolmesCo mineral survey.

title: Comparing groups
text:
The escalation



Number of tests
P(≥1 false positive)




1
5%


3
14%


10
40%


20
64%


100
99.4%




Run enough tests and you’re guaranteed to “find” something.

title: Comparing groups
text:
HolmesCo’s mineral survey


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Press Release — Multi-Element Anomaly Discovered!
“We tested soil samples from 20 sites for enrichment in 20 elements. We found statistically significant enrichment (p < 0.05) for three elements at one site!”



If there’s truly nothing there, how many significant results would you expect by chance?



20 elements × 20 sites = 400 tests × 0.05 = 20 expected false positives. Three hits is fewer than expected by chance. HolmesCo’s “discovery” actually underperforms random noise.

title: Comparing groups
text:
The answer
20 × 20 = 400 tests at α = 0.05
Expected false positives: 20
HolmesCo found 3.

That’s fewer than expected by chance. The “discovery” is noise.


Let this land. The subversion — that the result is actually less than chance — makes the multiple comparisons problem viscerally clear.

title: Comparing groups
text:
Your project

How many comparisons are you planning to make?


If you run all of them at α = 0.05, what’s your family-wise false positive rate?


Formula: 1 − (1 − α)k where k = number of tests


[10 min] Students calculate for their own projects. Then discuss: “If you’re testing more than one thing, you need to account for it.”

title: Comparing groups
text:
One test for multiple groups
ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) asks:

“Does any group differ from the others?”

Without specifying which.


[20 min block] ANOVA mechanics, live demo, post-hoc tests.

title: Comparing groups
text:
The logic

Total variation = between groups + within groups
If between-group variation is large relative to within-group → at least one group is different
The F-statistic captures this ratio
The p-value tells you how surprising this F is under the null

title: Comparing groups
text:
Live demo
model <- aov(output ~ region, data = wind_df)
summary(model)

If significant → which pairs differ?
TukeyHSD(model)


Live-code this in WebR. Show the output, read the pairwise comparisons. Point out the adjusted p-values.

title: Comparing groups
text:
Corrections for multiple comparisons








Method
How
When




Tukey’s HSD
All pairwise, designed for ANOVA
After a significant ANOVA


Bonferroni
Divide α by number of tests
Simple, conservative, any context




Both reduce the false positive rate by being stricter about what counts as significant.


Don’t go deep into the math. The key idea: when you test multiple things, you raise the bar for each individual test.

title: Comparing groups
text:
In WebR
Provided dataset: wind farm output in four regions.

Visualise with boxplots
Run aov()
Is it significant?
Run TukeyHSD() — which pairs differ?
Write a one-sentence conclusion



[15 min] Hands-on. The WebR exercise page has scaffolding.

title: Comparing groups
text: “Significant” ≠ “important”

title: Comparing groups
section: A p-value tells you whether an effect is detectable.
text: A p-value tells you whether an effect is detectable.

title: Comparing groups
section: It does not tell you whether it’s big enough to matter.
text:
It does not tell you whether it’s big enough to matter.


[20 min block] Effect sizes — the statistical formalisation of “Is that a big number?”

title: Comparing groups
text:
The HolmesCo temperature problem


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Two boreholes, 1,000 readings each.
Mean difference: 0.02°C. p-value: 0.001.
“Highly significant temperature anomaly detected!”


Is 0.02°C meaningful for any practical purpose?


With enough data, trivially small differences become “significant.” The p-value says “detectable,” not “important.”

title: Comparing groups
text:
Cohen’s d
A standardised measure of how far apart two groups are:



d
Interpretation




0.2
Small


0.5
Medium


0.8
Large




effectsize::cohens_d(y ~ group, data = df)


d = (mean₁ − mean₂) / pooled SD. It answers “how many standard deviations apart are the groups?”

title: Comparing groups
text:
For ANOVA: η² (eta-squared)
Proportion of variance explained by the grouping variable.



η²
Interpretation




0.01
Small


0.06
Medium


0.14
Large





Always report both the p-value and an effect size.
A policy-maker needs to know “how big?” — not just “is it real?”



Link to “Is that a big number?” from Week 2. Same habit, formalised.

title: Comparing groups
text:
Three test results


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Technical Summary

Test A: p = 0.002, Cohen’s d = 1.2
Test B: p = 0.04, Cohen’s d = 0.08
Test C: p = 0.08, 95% CI = [−0.3, 6.1]



For each: should we act on this? Why or why not?


[15 min] A is significant with a large effect — act. B is significant but trivially small — maybe not. C is non-significant but the CI almost excludes zero — worth investigating further.

title: Comparing groups
text:
Key points

Multiple tests inflate false positives — correct for them
ANOVA compares multiple groups in one test; TukeyHSD finds which differ
Significance ≠ importance — always report effect sizes
“Is that a big number?” now has a formal answer

title: Comparing groups
text:
Next time
Application session: “Deepening your analysis”
Apply ANOVA or refine your t-test with effect sizes and assumption checks.


Not all groups need ANOVA. Groups with two-group comparisons should deepen their t-test analysis with CIs and effect sizes.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Where we are
You can now test hypotheses, compare groups, and quantify effect sizes.

This week: models.
A model is a simplified story about how the world works. The question is always: how much does it capture, and what does it leave out?


[5 min] Frame the session: models as simplifications. Every equation in science is a model. Every test contains an implicit model.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Models and their limits
text:
What is a model?
A deliberate simplification that captures some features of reality and ignores others.

Every statistical test you’ve run contains an implicit model.
The t-test models data as two normal distributions with equal variance.
Now we make the model explicit.


[20 min block] Linear regression — concept and live demo.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Linear regression
\[y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon\]

“y increases by β₁ for every one-unit increase in x, plus noise.”


That’s the whole model. It’s powerful — and limited.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Live demo

🖥️ Switching to WebR

model <- lm(output ~ windspeed, data = turbine_df)
summary(model)


Show the output: coefficients, R², p-values. Plot with geom_point() + geom_smooth(method = "lm").
Interpret: “Wind speed explains ~60% of the variation. For each 1 m/s increase, output increases by ~X kWh.”

title: Models and their limits
text:
Reading the output



Element
Meaning




Intercept (β₀)
Predicted y when x = 0


Slope (β₁)
Change in y per unit change in x


R²
Proportion of variance explained


p-value (slope)
Is the slope distinguishable from zero?





Emphasise R² interpretation: “R² = 0.6 means 60% of the variation is explained. 40% is something else.”

title: Models and their limits
text:
Solar panels and temperature
Solar panel output vs temperature — counterintuitive.

Make a scatter plot
Fit lm()
Interpret the slope and R²
Plot the fit



Is the relationship linear? Does the model capture the pattern?



[15 min] Output decreases at high temperatures for crystalline silicon panels. Students discover a negative slope where they expected positive. Good moment to ask “Did the data surprise you?”

title: Models and their limits
text:
The four assumptions

Linearity — the relationship is actually linear
Independence — residuals don’t influence each other
Homoscedasticity — constant variance of residuals
Normality of residuals (for inference)



[20 min block] Assumptions, diagnostic plots, what to do when violated.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Diagnostic plots
plot(model)
Four plots, two that matter most:







Plot
What to look for




Residuals vs Fitted
Pattern = non-linearity or heteroscedasticity


QQ plot
Departures from the line = non-normal residuals





Walk through each plot. “No pattern in residuals vs fitted = good. Points on the QQ line = good.”

title: Models and their limits
text:
The scenario


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

Groundwater Analysis Report
Linear model: groundwater level ~ rainfall. R² = 0.45.
“Rainfall is the dominant control on groundwater levels.”


Now look at the diagnostic plots…


[15 min] Show diagnostic plots with a clear seasonal pattern in the residuals. There’s a time-lag and seasonal cycle the model isn’t capturing.

title: Models and their limits
text:
What’s wrong?

The residuals show a seasonal pattern — the model is missing a time-dependent structure
R² = 0.45 means 55% of the variation is unexplained
“Dominant” is a stretch when your model misses more than it captures



Key question: what is the model not capturing? (Seasonal recharge, time lag between rainfall and groundwater response, temperature effects on evapotranspiration.)

title: Models and their limits
text:
Failure 1: Overfitting
A model that fits the training data perfectly but fails on new data.
It’s memorised the noise.


[25 min block] Three failures — each a different form of hubris. Demo the polynomial overfitting live.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Demo: polynomial degree 15

🖥️ Live demo

Fit a degree-15 polynomial to 20 data points. It goes through every point but oscillates wildly between them.
Compare to the straight line: fits worse, predicts better.


More complex ≠ more useful.



Live-code: geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 15)) vs y ~ x on the same plot. The visual contrast is striking.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Failure 2: Extrapolation
A model that works within the observed range but breaks down outside it.

Example: UK solar capacity has grown roughly exponentially since 2010. Extrapolate to 2050 → solar exceeds the entire UK grid.


What’s the model not capturing?
Planning constraints, grid limits, diminishing suitable sites.

title: Models and their limits
text:
HolmesCo’s extrapolation


HolmesCo
“Geological Solutions Since 2019”

“Our linear trend in groundwater decline predicts the aquifer will be empty by 2028.”
They didn’t account for seasonal recharge or the fact that the decline was caused by temporary pumping.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Failure 3: Blind spots
Things the model structurally cannot represent.


A single-turbine power curve has no term for wake interference → overpredicts wind farm output by 10–20%
A carbon accounting model that counts biomass as zero structurally cannot see the emissions it ignores




You can only fix a blind spot if you know it’s there.

title: Models and their limits
text: The new refrain

title: Models and their limits
text:
“What is the model not capturing?”
Overfitting: too much faith in your data.
Extrapolation: too much faith in your model.
Blind spots: too much faith in your framework.


These are three forms of the same mistake: confusing the map for the territory. Each is a different flavour of hubris.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Write one sentence

“In my project, the biggest thing my analysis might be missing is…”



[5 min] Quick written reflection. Students keep this for their reports.

title: Models and their limits
text:
Key points

A model is a simplification — powerful, but limited
Always check the diagnostics: residuals don’t lie
Three failures: overfitting, extrapolation, blind spots
Always ask: “What is my model not capturing?”

title: Models and their limits
text:
Next time
Application session: “Fitting and breaking models”
You’ll fit models to your data — and deliberately try to break them.


The application session includes both project work and a structured extrapolation exercise that everyone does.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Where we are
Your projects are nearly done. You’ve designed investigations, run tests, fitted models, and identified limitations.

This week: two forces that shape modern research —
AI and the pressure to produce results.


[5 min] This session is more discursive than technical — more debate and reflection, less coding.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
AI is good at…

Generating first-draft code
Summarising text
Suggesting structure
Spotting syntax errors
Explaining error messages



[20 min block] Honest assessment of AI capabilities and limits.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
AI is bad at…

Knowing whether its answer is correct
Understanding your specific data
Making judgement calls about methodology
Citing sources reliably
Distinguishing meaningful from spurious results

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Live demo (if possible)

🖥️ Give an AI the HolmesCo gold assay scenario
“We tested 10,000 soil samples with a 95%-accurate assay. 50 tested positive. What’s the probability of a true gold deposit?”


Watch what it does with the base rate.


The AI will likely either get it wrong (ignoring the base rate) or give a generically correct but vague answer without applying it to the specific numbers.
Have a backup screenshot in case the AI gives a perfect answer.
The point: fluent ≠ thoughtful.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
The key distinction

AI is fluent but not thoughtful.
It can write a convincing paragraph about any result — including a wrong one.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Your experience

Think about a time in this module when you used AI (or were tempted to). What did it help with? Where did you have to override it?


Common patterns:

Useful: debugging code, plot templates, explaining errors
Unreliable: interpreting results, choosing methods



[15 min] Open discussion or think-pair-share. Collect themes on the board. Students are generally honest about this — the goal is reflection, not confession.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
The replication crisis
Many published findings don’t hold up when others try to reproduce them.


[20 min block] Reproducibility as a practical skill.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Why?

p-hacking — trying many analyses, reporting the one that “works”
Underpowered studies — too few observations to detect real effects
Selective reporting — filing away null results
Data not shared — no one can check your work

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
section: Your commit history IS your reproducibility record
text:
Your commit history IS your reproducibility record

If someone cloned your repo and ran your code, would they get the same results?

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Practical reproducibility habits

Script everything — no “I typed this in the console”
Document your decisions — why this test, why you excluded those data points
Record software versions — sessionInfo() in R
Share data and code — within ethical limits



Connection to the policy report: a policy-maker should be able to trace your conclusion back to your data.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Can you re-run your analysis?
Open your project repo. Start a fresh R session.
Run your analysis script from scratch.


Does it work? What breaks?

Common problems:

Hardcoded file paths
Missing libraries
Steps done in the console but not saved



Fix what you can. 10 minutes.


[15 min] This is surprisingly powerful. Most students will find at least one thing that breaks. That experience is more persuasive than any lecture.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
The spectrum


[20 min block] Brief and frank. Not preachy.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Three levels








Level
What
Example




Innocent errors
Rounding, wrong column, misread output
Your peer reviewer catches these


Questionable practices
p-hacking, HARKing, selective reporting
Often unintentional — this is what base rate and multiple comparisons sessions were about


Fabrication
Making up or altering data
Rare, career-ending, easier to detect than you’d think

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Where does AI fit?
If AI writes your analysis and you don’t check it → you are responsible for the errors.

If AI generates data or figures that don’t reflect reality → that’s fabrication, even if unintentional.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
The HolmesCo connection
HolmesCo doesn’t fabricate data.

But their corner-cutting on design, analysis, and interpretation produces conclusions that are functionally no better than fabrication.



Integrity isn’t just “don’t lie.”
It’s “do the work properly.”

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Your questions
This is a looser session. What’s on your mind?

Questions about your reports?
AI use policies?
What counts as plagiarism when AI is involved?



[10 min] Address what comes up. Students often have genuine questions about the AI boundary. The honest answer: use AI as a tool, check everything, and document what you used.

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Key points

AI is useful for mechanics; unreliable for judgement
Reproducibility = scripted, documented, version-controlled
Integrity = doing the work properly, not just not lying

title: AI, reproducibility, and integrity
text:
Next time
Application session: “AI as critic”
You’ll feed your analysis to an AI and evaluate its feedback.
The question: how much should you trust it?


Students should bring their analysis code and a draft results section to the application session.

title: Bringing it together
text:
The full journey
You’ve designed an investigation, collected and analysed data, checked assumptions, fitted models, and identified limitations.

Now: write it up so someone else can understand it and act on it.


[5 min] This is the last content delivery. It should leave students confident about what a good report looks like.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Questions?










Submit questions anonymously:
PollEv.com/geol
text geol to 07480 781235





Open the Poll Everywhere Q&A activity before the session and leave it active throughout.
Questions appear in your Poll Everywhere presenter view; upvoted questions rise to the top.
Address during transitions or at the end of concept blocks, not mid-explanation.
Reset (archive) the activity between sessions so questions don’t carry over from the previous week.

title: Bringing it together
text:
The 4-page policy report








Section
Length
Purpose




Summary
½ page
The finding, the evidence, the recommendation


Introduction
½ page
The question, why it matters


Methods
½–1 page
Data, approach, why these tests


Results
1–1½ pages
Figures, tests, plain-language interpretation


Discussion
1 page
What it means, caveats, recommendation





[20 min block] Walk through each section using the Phase 1 faithful exemplar as a concrete example.

title: Bringing it together
text:
The summary test (revisited)
A reader who stops after the summary should still know:

What you investigated
What you found
What you recommend


If any of these are missing, the summary needs work.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Methods: enough to reproduce
Your methods section should answer:

Where did the data come from?
What did you do to clean/prepare it?
Which tests, and why?
What software and versions?



Could someone clone your repo and replicate your analysis?

title: Bringing it together
section: Results: figures that tell a story
text:
Results: figures that tell a story
Every figure should have:

A clear “so what”
A caption that tells the reader what to see
Axis labels with units
A reference in the text


Every test result should include:

The test used
The p-value and an effect size or CI
A plain-language interpretation

title: Bringing it together
text:
Trade summaries
Swap your draft summary with someone from a different group.

As a reader, answer:

What is the main finding?
Do I trust it?
What would I do with this information?




If the reader can’t answer these from the summary alone, it needs work.



[15 min] Students write (if they haven’t) or refine, then swap and discuss.

title: Bringing it together
text:
How it works
Submit → Editor assigns reviewers → Anonymous critique → Revise and resubmit.


[20 min block] Peer review as self-correction mechanism.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Why it matters

It’s how science self-corrects
Catches errors the authors missed
Challenges assumptions
Improves clarity

title: Bringing it together
text:
Its weaknesses

Slow
Biased toward established researchers and ideas
Doesn’t catch fraud well
Can be superficial

title: Bringing it together
text:
What makes a good review

Specific — not “this could be better” but “the CI is missing from Table 2”
Constructive — what to fix, not just what’s wrong
Focused on argument and evidence — not just grammar


Your Week 4 GitHub Issues review was peer review. You’ll do it again now, with higher standards.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Quick-fire: spot the problem
Six excerpts from fictional reports. Two minutes each.


[15 min] These are cumulative — each pitfall connects to a specific week’s content. Students should recognise most of them.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 1

“The data clearly demonstrate that offshore wind is the most cost-effective energy source.”


Problem: Conclusion not supported by the data shown. The report only compared three sources and used levelised cost from 2019.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 2
A figure with no caption, no axis labels, and no reference in the text.

Problem: The reader can’t interpret it. A figure that isn’t discussed is decoration, not evidence.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 3

“p = 0.04, therefore the effect is significant and the policy should be adopted.”


Problem: No effect size, no CI, no discussion of practical significance. “Is that a big number?”

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 4

“The results prove that solar power will eliminate fossil fuel use by 2040.”


Problem: Overstatement. “Prove” is almost never appropriate. Extrapolation without caveats.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 5

“We used statistical analysis to test the hypothesis.”


Problem: Methods too vague to reproduce. Which test? Why? On what data?

title: Bringing it together
text:
Pitfall 6
A report with no limitations section.

Problem: Every analysis has limitations. Omitting them doesn’t make them disappear — it makes the reader distrust you.

title: Bringing it together
text:
The key habits
These are the questions that run through the whole course:


[20 min block] Tie the whole arc together. Each refrain came from a specific week.

title: Bringing it together
text: The refrains

title: Bringing it together
section: “Is that a big number?” / “Compared to what?”
text:
“Is that a big number?” / “Compared to what?”
Week 2

title: Bringing it together
text:
“What assumptions are we making?”
Week 3

title: Bringing it together
section: “How plausible was this before we tested?”
text:
“How plausible was this before we tested?”
Week 6

title: Bringing it together
text:
“What is the model not capturing?”
Week 8

title: Bringing it together
section: The analyst you want to be
text:
The analyst you want to be

A good report uses these questions reflexively.
A HolmesCo report ignores them.
Your goal: be the analyst whose work a policy-maker can trust.



This is the emotional capstone. Students have learned these habits through 10 weeks of practice. Name them explicitly and show that each came from a specific moment in the course.

title: Bringing it together
text:
The process
In the application session:

Finalise your draft (20 min)
Review someone else’s report from a different group (40 min)
File 3–4 GitHub Issues using the updated checklist
Debrief and revise (30 min)


The review template is more demanding than Week 4’s — it now includes effect sizes, assumptions, reproducibility, and overstatement checks.


Distribute review assignments now or at the start of the application session.

title: Bringing it together
text:
Next time
Application session: “Review, revise, submit”
Bring a complete draft. Whatever is committed at minute 30 gets reviewed.

This is the last session. Make it count.


The stakes are clear. Students should arrive with a near-complete draft.

title: HolmesCo: Gold Assay
text:
This is a fictional document for teaching purposes.




⚒️ HolmesCo


Geological Consultants Since 2019



PRESS RELEASE — FOR IMMEDIATE DISTRIBUTION
Date: March 2026


HolmesCo is pleased to announce the results of a landmark geochemical survey across County Durham and the Northern Pennines.


Our field teams collected soil and shallow rock samples from 10,000 locations across the survey area. Each sample was tested using HolmesCo’s proprietary AuDetect™ geochemical assay, which identifies gold-bearing rock with 95% accuracy:

If gold is present, the assay correctly identifies it 95% of the time (sensitivity)
If gold is absent, the assay correctly rules it out 95% of the time (specificity)




Of the 10,000 samples tested, 50 returned positive results for gold mineralisation.
The positive samples are concentrated in the western part of the survey area, consistent with known geological structures in the Alston Block.




“Fifty confirmed gold hits across County Durham represents a highly significant discovery. Our AuDetect™ assay is 95% accurate — these results give us great confidence that there is genuine gold mineralisation in the region. We recommend immediate follow-up drilling at the most promising sites.”
— Dr G. Holmes, Chief Scientific Officer, HolmesCo

HolmesCo has applied for three exploration licences and is seeking investment partners to fund a Phase 2 drilling programme.
Investors interested in this opportunity should contact HolmesCo’s Business Development team.


This press release has been prepared by HolmesCo and has not been independently reviewed. AuDetect™ accuracy figures are based on laboratory validation against known reference samples. Field performance may vary. HolmesCo accepts no liability for investment decisions made on the basis of this announcement.






The base rate destroys HolmesCo’s claim.
This handout goes with the base rate / conditional probability segment in Week 6 content (concept block 3). Present the handout first, then guide students through the arithmetic.


“HolmesCo says the assay is 95% accurate and they found 50 positives. How many of those 50 are likely to be real gold?”
Most students will initially guess around 47–48 (95% of 50).



Ask: “How common is gold in County Durham?”
Gold mineralisation in the Northern Pennines is rare. A generous estimate is about 5 in 10,000 samples (0.05%) actually contain gold-bearing rock.
Work through a natural frequency table for 10,000 samples:










Truly gold-bearing (5)
Not gold-bearing (9,995)
Total




Assay positive
5 × 0.95 ≈ 5
9,995 × 0.05 ≈ 500
~505


Assay negative
5 × 0.05 ≈ 0
9,995 × 0.95 ≈ 9,495
~9,495



With a 95%-accurate test and a base rate of 0.05%, you’d expect roughly 505 positive results, of which only 5 are real.
P(gold | positive) = 5 / 505 ≈ 1%.
A positive result from HolmesCo’s assay means there is about a 1 in 100 chance the sample actually contains gold.



The maths predicts ~505 positives, but HolmesCo only found 50. This is actually fewer than expected from false positives alone (~500). Two possible explanations:

The assay’s specificity is slightly better than 95% in the field (say 99.5%), which would give ~50 false positives — consistent with HolmesCo’s results.
HolmesCo applied additional screening or filtering (and didn’t mention it in the press release).

Either way, the positive predictive value remains terrible. Even in the best case (99.5% specificity, 50 positives total), only ~5 would be real: PPV ≈ 5/55 ≈ 9%. Of HolmesCo’s 50 “confirmed” hits, probably 45–50 are false.




P(data | H₀) ≠ P(H₀ | data). The assay is 95% accurate (P(positive | gold) = 0.95). But what we need is P(gold | positive), which depends on the base rate. Students confuse these — that’s exactly the point.
The base rate matters enormously. Gold is rare. When the thing you’re looking for is rare, even a very accurate test produces mostly false positives.
“95% accurate” sounds reassuring but is misleading. HolmesCo’s press release trades on the intuition that 95% accuracy ≈ 95% chance of being right. It isn’t, when the base rate is low.
Connection to p-values. A p-value tells you P(data | H₀) — how surprising the result is if the null is true. But what you want is P(H₀ | data) — is the null actually true given your data? The difference depends on the base rate: how plausible was the hypothesis before you tested? Testing an implausible hypothesis (gold in Durham) at α = 0.05 is the p-value equivalent of HolmesCo’s 95%-accurate assay on a 0.05% base rate.
“How plausible was this before we tested?” This is the new refrain, joining “Is that a big number?” Students should develop the habit of asking about prior plausibility before interpreting any test result.




“When you run a t-test in your project next week, what’s your prior expectation? Is the effect you’re looking for plausible or a long shot? How should that change how you interpret the p-value?”



Before revealing the HolmesCo numbers, you may want to run the classic medical test version:

A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people
A test is 99% sensitive, 99% specific
You test positive — what’s the probability you have the disease?

Answer: ~9% (not 99%). This builds the intuition; the HolmesCo version then cements it with a geological example that sticks.

title: HolmesCo: Mineral Survey
text:
This is a fictional document for teaching purposes.




⚒️ HolmesCo


Geological Consultants Since 2019



PRESS RELEASE — FOR IMMEDIATE DISTRIBUTION
Date: March 2026


In response to the UK government’s Critical Minerals Strategy, HolmesCo has completed a comprehensive geochemical survey targeting minerals essential for the clean energy transition.
Our field teams collected soil samples from 20 sites spanning County Durham, Northumberland, Cumbria, and North Yorkshire. Each sample was analysed for enrichment in 20 critical minerals including lithium, cobalt, tungsten, niobium, and rare earth elements.
Using rigorous statistical testing at the industry-standard significance level (p < 0.05), we identified statistically significant enrichment for three critical minerals at one site near Alston, Cumbria.

“With global demand for critical minerals surging, this is a strategically important discovery. Three separate minerals showing significant enrichment at the same location strongly suggests a genuine mineralisation event — right here in the North Pennines.”
— Dr G. Holmes, Chief Scientific Officer, HolmesCo

HolmesCo recommends immediate follow-up drilling at the Alston site and has applied for an exploration licence under the Crown Estate’s critical minerals framework.
Investors interested in supporting UK mineral security should contact HolmesCo’s Business Development team.




The numbers tell a different story.
Ask the class to work through the arithmetic:

20 sites × 20 minerals = 400 tests
At α = 0.05, each test has a 5% false positive rate
Expected false positives (if nothing is there): 400 × 0.05 = 20
HolmesCo found 3 significant results

Three hits is fewer than expected by chance. Far from being evidence of a “genuine mineralisation event,” the result is actually underwhelming — you’d expect about 20 false positives from 400 tests, and they only found 3.
Key teaching points:

The multiple comparisons problem. Every test you run is another chance for a false positive. Running 400 tests at α = 0.05 is almost guaranteed to produce “significant” results even when nothing is real.
The family-wise error rate. P(at least one false positive) = 1 − 0.95400 ≈ 1.00 (virtually certain). HolmesCo’s individual p < 0.05 threshold is meaningless without correction.
Three hits < 20 expected — the result is actually evidence against a real discovery, not for one. This subverts students’ expectation that significance = discovery.
The press release framing. Notice how “three critical minerals at one site” sounds impressive — especially when wrapped in language about national strategy and the energy transition. The total number of tests (400) is not mentioned. This is a common pattern in misleading statistical claims: report the hits, hide the denominator. The policy urgency around critical minerals makes the reader want to believe it.

Discussion question: How would you correct for multiple comparisons?

Bonferroni correction: divide α by the number of tests. α_corrected = 0.05 / 400 = 0.000125. None of HolmesCo’s results would survive this correction.
ANOVA approach: test all sites simultaneously for each mineral, then use post-hoc tests with correction (like TukeyHSD).
Simply reporting the number of tests alongside the results would let the reader judge for themselves.

Three hits < 20 expected — the result is actually evidence against a real discovery, not for one. This subverts students’ expectation that significance = discovery.
The press release framing. Notice how “three critical minerals at one site” sounds impressive — especially when wrapped in language about national strategy and the energy transition. The total number of tests (400) is not mentioned. This is a common pattern in misleading statistical claims: report the hits, hide the denominator. The policy urgency around critical minerals makes the reader want to believe it.

Discussion question: How would you correct for multiple comparisons?

Bonferroni correction: divide α by the number of tests. α_corrected = 0.05 / 400 = 0.000125. None of HolmesCo’s results would survive this correction.
ANOVA approach: test all sites simultaneously for each mineral, then use post-hoc tests with correction (like TukeyHSD).
Simply reporting the number of tests alongside the results would let the reader judge for themselves.

title: HolmesCo: Site Investigation
text:
This is a fictional document for teaching purposes. Any resemblance to real consultancy reports is deliberate and cautionary.




⚒️ HolmesCo


Geological Consultants Since 2019


“Building Confidence in the Ground Beneath You”™



GROUND INVESTIGATION REPORT
Prepared for: Weardale Wind Developments Ltd
Project: Proposed Middlehope Wind Farm, County Durham
Report No.: HC-2026-0347
Date: February 2026
Classification: CONFIDENTIAL



HolmesCo was appointed by Weardale Wind Developments Ltd to undertake a ground investigation at the proposed Middlehope Wind Farm site, located on high ground between Weardale and Teesdale in County Durham (NGR NY 890 350, approximate).
The purpose of the investigation was to determine whether the bedrock is suitable to support wind turbine foundations.



Three boreholes were drilled to a depth of 15 metres each:



Borehole
Location
Elevation (m AOD)




BH-01
Valley floor, Middlehope Burn
285


BH-02
Valley floor, 200m south of BH-01
288


BH-03
Low terrace, east bank of burn
292



Drilling was carried out by HolmesCo’s in-house rotary rig between 8–10 January 2026. Continuous core was recovered from all three boreholes. Standard penetration tests (SPTs) were conducted at 1.5m intervals.
Borehole locations were selected to ensure safe and efficient access for the drill rig, which requires a level platform and proximity to a track for support vehicles.



All three boreholes encountered the following sequence:

0–2 m: Glacial till (firm to stiff clay with gravel)
2–15 m: Sandstone (Stainmore Formation), moderately strong to strong, thinly to medium bedded, occasional thin mudstone interbeds

SPT N-values in the sandstone ranged from 35 to 50+ (refusal), indicating competent rock.
No groundwater was encountered during drilling. No significant discontinuities or zones of weakness were observed in the recovered core.



Based on the results of this investigation, the bedrock at the Middlehope Wind Farm site comprises competent sandstone of the Stainmore Formation throughout. The site is considered suitable for wind turbine foundations subject to detailed foundation design.
HolmesCo recommends proceeding to the detailed design phase with confidence.


This report has been prepared by HolmesCo for the exclusive use of Weardale Wind Developments Ltd and should not be relied upon by third parties. HolmesCo accepts no liability for losses arising from use of this report by others. Standard terms and conditions apply.





What students should identify:

All boreholes are in the valley (285–292 m AOD). Wind turbines will be sited on the ridgetops (typically 500–600 m AOD in this area). The investigation doesn’t test the ground where it matters.
Three boreholes is a very small sample for a site that may span several square kilometres with varying geology. The Stainmore Formation includes both sandstone and mudstone — three valley-floor boreholes can’t characterise the full range.
Selection bias / convenience sampling. The report explicitly states that “borehole locations were selected to ensure safe and efficient access.” This is convenience sampling dressed up as methodology.
No control or comparison. What does “competent” mean without context? No reference to design loading, no comparison to similar sites, no indication of acceptable vs unacceptable SPT values for turbine foundations.
Overconfident conclusion. “Throughout” implies the entire site has been characterised; in reality, three points in one small area have been tested. The recommendation to “proceed with confidence” is not supported by the evidence.

Discussion prompt: What would a proper investigation look like? (Boreholes on the ridgetops, at proposed turbine locations; enough boreholes to capture geological variability; a stratified sampling design; reference to foundation design requirements.)
